1: The date header establishes that this oracle against Tyre was delivered in the same period as the fall of Jerusalem (the eleventh year, though the month is missing in the Hebrew — possibly a scribal gap). The historical coincidence is theologically significant: just as YHWH judges his own covenant city, he also judges the city that gloated over its fall. In Christ, this double-judgment structure is resolved: the cross is both the judgment of Jerusalem's sin (Matthew 23:37-38; Luke 19:41-44) and the defeat of the world's proud systems (John 12:31: “now the ruler of this world will be cast out”).
2: Tyre's sin is gloating over Jerusalem's fall: “Aha! The gate of the peoples is broken; it has swung open to me.” The commercial city sees Jerusalem's destruction as an opportunity — trade routes through the Levantine corridor would be redirected through Tyre. Christ's parable of the tenants (Matthew 21:33-44) echoes this dynamic: those who exploit the owner's absence to seize the inheritance will face the owner's return. Tyre's calculation — Jerusalem's loss is Tyre's gain — mirrors every worldly scheme that profits from the suffering of others. The same logic underlies Edom's treachery (Ezekiel 25:12; Obad 11-14). Christ's judgment reverses this inversion.
3: “Many nations against you as the sea brings up its waves” — YHWH's judgment on Tyre comes in waves, not a single blow: Nebuchadnezzar's 13-year siege (585-573 BCE), then Alexander's causeway conquest (332 BCE), then subsequent decline through the Hellenistic and Roman periods. This cumulative judgment anticipates the eschatological pattern in Revelation: the judgments come in sequences (seals, trumpets, bowls), each wave deeper than the last. Christ is the one who “rides on the waves” (Matthew 14:25-26) — he is the Lord of the seas whose waves of judgment no earthly power can withstand.
4: “I will also scrape the soil from her and make her a bare rock” — the stripping of Tyre to bare rock is the judgment of exposure: what lay under the beauty and commerce is simply stone. Alexander the Great literally scraped the mainland rubble of Old Tyre into the sea to build his causeway to the island fortress in 332 BCE, leaving the mainland site bare — one of the most precise fulfillments of prophetic detail in ancient history. Christologically, the cross is the ultimate “bare rock” moment: the world's pretensions stripped away, nothing left but the bare truth of divine judgment and grace. Christ himself is the “rock” (1 Corinthians 10:4; Matthew 7:24-25) — but it is not bare stone for destruction; it is the foundation of life.
5: “She will become a place in the middle of the sea for the spreading of nets” — the prophetic image is of fishermen using the ruins of the great city as a platform for drying fishing nets. The fishing imagery recalls Jesus's call of the disciples: “Follow me, and I will make you fishers of men” (Matthew 4:19). Where Tyre spread commercial wealth across the seas, the disciples would spread the gospel net. The contrast is instructive: Tyre's nets caught material goods; the gospel net catches human souls. The place where commercial empire falls becomes the place from which the kingdom of God spreads.
6: “Her daughters on the mainland will be slain by the sword, and they shall know that I am the LORD” — the “daughters” are the dependent mainland settlements. The knowledge formula that follows every judgment announces the epistemological outcome: YHWH's identity is disclosed through his acts of judgment. Christ is the ultimate disclosure of YHWH's identity — not through judgment but through the cross and resurrection. The “they will know that I am the LORD” formula finds its NT fulfillment in Philippians 2:10-11: “at the name of Jesus every knee shall bow and every tongue confess that Jesus Christ is Lord” — universal acknowledgment through the revelation of Christ's lordship.
7: Nebuchadnezzar is described as “king of kings” (melek hamelachim) — the supreme title of ANE monarchy, used also for Artaxerxes (Ezra 7:12) and in the NT as Christ's title (Revelation 17:14; Ezekiel 19:16: “King of kings and Lord of lords”). The use of “king of kings” for Nebuchadnezzar, the temporary human instrument of divine judgment, is itself an implicit pointing beyond him: the true King of kings is the one who employs Nebuchadnezzar as his sword. Christ's title “King of kings” in Revelation asserts that what Nebuchadnezzar was called by flattery, Christ is by nature.
8: The detailed siege terminology (siege works, mound, siege engines, shields) describes the literal mechanics of Babylonian military conquest. Christ's conquest is accomplished by entirely opposite means: not with battering rams against walls but with the “sword of the Spirit, which is the word of God” (Ephesians 6:17), not by force but by the power of resurrection life (2 Corinthians 10:4-5: “the weapons of our warfare are not carnal but mighty in God for pulling down strongholds”). The contrast between Nebuchadnezzar's military siege and Christ's gospel conquest is the contrast between the first and last kingdoms.
9: Battering rams against walls and towers broken down — the progressive dismantling of Tyre's defenses mirrors the progressive revelation that no human security system is permanent. Hebrews 12:26-27 applies the shaking of earthly structures explicitly to Christ's kingdom: “this phrase, 'Yet once more,' indicates the removing of things that are shaken ... in order that the things that cannot be shaken may remain.” The walls of Tyre are among the “shakeable” things; Christ's kingdom is the “unshakeable” one that remains after all earthly powers are leveled.
10: “Your walls will shake at the noise of horsemen, wheels, and chariots when he enters your gates” — the overwhelming military force that causes walls to tremble contrasts with Christ's entry into Jerusalem on a donkey (Matthew 21:5: “your king is coming to you, humble and mounted on a donkey”, citing Zechariah 9:9). The King of kings entered the world's great city not with the noise of war-horses but in deliberate humility. Yet this humble entry led to the judgment that Zechariah 9:9-10 itself anticipated: the dismantling of war-horses and chariots and the proclamation of peace “to the nations.”
11: “His horses' hooves will trample all your streets; he will slaughter your people with the sword and your strong pillars will collapse to the ground” — the strong pillars (matsevot oz) were Tyre's commemorative stelae and victory monuments. In Revelation 3:12 Christ promises to make the overcomer “a pillar in the temple of my God, never to go out of it” — the pillars that earthly powers erect and YHWH tears down are replaced with human beings incorporated permanently into the divine temple. The judgment of Tyre's pillars clears the way for a different kind of architectural permanence.
12: “They will cast your stones, your timber, and your soil into the sea” — this verse is strikingly fulfilled by Alexander's 332 BCE causeway: he used the ruins of mainland Tyre (stones, timber, and soil) as construction material, dragging them into the sea to build the road to the island city. The historical precision of this prophecy (verified by ancient sources including Arrian's Anabasis) is one of the strongest arguments for the predictive character of biblical prophecy. Christ fulfills prophecy not in hidden, allegorized ways but in sometimes startlingly concrete historical events that vindicate the prophetic claim.
13: “I will put an end to the noise of your songs, and the sound of your harps will be heard no more” — the silencing of Tyre's music is the direct source for Revelation 18:22: “the sound of harpists and musicians, of flute players and trumpeters, will be heard in you no more.” In Revelation 18, the fallen “Babylon” is a composite of every proud commercial civilization including Tyre. Christ's final judgment silences the music of worldly self-celebration — but it is simultaneously replaced by the “new song” of Revelation 5:9 and Ezekiel 14:3, the song of the redeemed before the Lamb. One music ends; another begins.
14: “You will be a place for the spreading of nets and will never be rebuilt” — modern Tyre (Sur in Lebanon) does exist as a city, which has led to debate about this verse. The most precise interpretation is that the ancient Tyre known for its commercial glory was permanently ended — the island fortress that Alexander conquered never recovered its previous stature. Christ's fulfillment of Ezekiel's judgment-oracles is not always through direct historical completion but through the principle they embody: no worldly system that opposes God's purposes will ultimately stand. The “never be rebuilt” points to the eschatological finality of Christ's judgment (Revelation 18:21: “Babylon the great city will be thrown down with violence and will be found no more”).
15: “Will not the coastlands tremble at the sound of your fall?” — the international trembling at Tyre's fall is the “fear of the LORD” motif made global: when YHWH acts in judgment, the nations recognize his power. Christ's cross and resurrection had the same effect — “the earth shook and the rocks split ... the centurion ... said, 'Surely he was the Son of God!'” (Matthew 27:51-54). The trembling of the nations at divine action is consistent from Tyre's fall to the crucifixion to the final judgment.
16: “All the princes of the sea will descend from their thrones, remove their robes, and strip off their embroidered garments; they will clothe themselves with trembling” — this mourning gesture of the sea-princes is directly cited in Revelation 18:9-10: “the kings of the earth who committed immorality and lived in luxury with her, they will weep and wail over her ... standing far off ... saying, 'Alas! Alas! You great city, you mighty city, Babylon!'” The voluntary demotion of princes (removing robes, sitting on the ground) reflects the shock of seeing that even the mightiest commercial empire can fall. Before Christ's judgment seat, all earthly thrones are equally temporary.
17: The lament: “How you are destroyed, you who were inhabited by seafarers — you renowned city, mighty on the sea!” — the mourning over Tyre anticipates the mourning over Babylon in Rev 18 but also the mourning over Jerusalem that Jesus himself initiated (Matthew 23:37-38; Luke 19:41-44). Jesus wept over Jerusalem with the same grief that Ezekiel's sea-princes show over Tyre — but with the additional dimension that he was simultaneously pronouncing and bearing the judgment. The one who laments the fall of the great city is also the one who executes the judgment that causes the fall.
18: “Now the coastlands tremble on the day of your fall; the islands in the sea are dismayed at your end” — the ripple effect of Tyre's judgment across the maritime world. In Christ's first coming, the response to his death and resurrection created exactly this kind of international trembling: from Jerusalem to Judea to Samaria to the ends of the earth (Acts 1:8), the impact of the cross spread outward like waves from the center. The “islands” (iyim) of the prophets are often the distant Gentile territories (Isaiah 41:1; Ezekiel 42:4; Ezekiel 49:1) — the nations at the ends of the earth who will ultimately be brought to the knowledge of YHWH.
19: “When I bring up the deep over you and the great waters cover you” — the primordial waters (tehom) covering Tyre evokes the flood narrative (Genesis 7:11: “all the springs of the great deep burst forth”) and reverses the creation narrative (the waters were restrained at creation so dry land appeared; here they are released again in un-creation). Christ's death is described in similar de-creation language: darkness covered the land (Matthew 27:45; Amos 8:9), the temple curtain tore (Matthew 27:51), the earth shook. The judgment of Tyre's un-creation is a type of the new creation that comes through Christ's death and resurrection — the old order passing away to make way for the new.
20: “I will bring you down with those who descend to the pit, to the ancient peoples ... but I will set glory in the land of the living” — this verse contains an extraordinary juxtaposition: while Tyre descends to the pit with the dead, YHWH will “set glory in the land of the living.” The glory (tzvi) set in the land of the living while the proud go to the pit is the resurrection-reversal: Christ descends to the realm of the dead (1 Peter 3:19; Acts 2:27 citing Psalms 16:10: “you will not abandon my soul to Sheol”) but is set in glory — the land of the living — through the resurrection. The contrast between Tyre's permanent descent and the glory established in the land of the living is ultimately the contrast between death and resurrection.
21: “You will be no more. Though you are sought, you will never be found again” — the permanent disappearance of Tyre from the stage of history finds its eschatological completion in Revelation 18:21: “Babylon the great city will be thrown down with violence and will be found no more forever.” In Christ, the “will never be found again” verdict applies to every power that opposes God: “the last enemy to be destroyed is death” (1 Corinthians 15:26); “and the devil who had deceived them was thrown into the lake of fire” (Revelation 20:10). What YHWH declared about Tyre is the template for the final verdict on every force of pride and opposition.
